questions.question: Which syndrome results from the compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery?
questions.detail: May-Thurner Syndrome is defined as a DVT in the left common iliac vein caused by compression from the right common iliac artery.

questions.question: What is the recommended treatment for May-Thurner Syndrome when presenting as an acute swollen leg?
questions.detail: Treatment for May-Thurner typically involves thrombolysis and stenting to address the venous obstruction.

questions.question: What is the most common peripheral arterial aneurysm overall?
questions.detail: The popliteal aneurysm is the most common peripheral arterial aneurysm and the second most common overall after the aorta.

questions.question: What percentage of patients with a popliteal artery aneurysm also have an abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)?
questions.detail: There is a strong association where 30-50% of patients with popliteal aneurysms have a concurrent AAA.

questions.question: What is considered the most dreaded complication of a popliteal artery aneurysm?
choices: Acute limb ischemia from distal thromboembolism
questions.detail: The main issue and most dreaded complication is an acute limb caused by distal thromboembolism.

questions.question: Which muscle is typically involved in the developmental relationship that causes "Popliteal Entrapment"?
questions.detail: Popliteal entrapment is caused by the medial deviation of the popliteal artery due to its relationship with the medial head of the gastrocnemius.

questions.question: In which patient population does "Cystic Adventitial Disease" classically occur?
questions.detail: This uncommon disorder classically affects the popliteal artery of young men.

questions.question: How does Cystic Adventitial Disease appear on fluid-sensitive MRI sequences?
choices: A bunch of cysts around the vessel extrinsically narrowing it
questions.detail: On MRI, it is seen as multiple mucoid-filled cysts developing in the outer media and adventitia that compress the artery.

questions.question: "Paget-Schroetter Syndrome" is associated with venous thrombosis in which vessel?
questions.detail: Paget-Schroetter involves the development of a venous thrombus in the subclavian vein.

questions.question: What is the classic nickname for Paget-Schroetter Syndrome?
questions.detail: It is often called "effort thrombosis" because it is associated with athletes who raise their arms frequently.

questions.question: Klippel-Trenaunay Syndrome (KTS) is characterized by port wine nevi, soft tissue/bony hypertrophy, and which vascular finding?
questions.detail: The marginal vein of Servelle, a large saphenous vein on the lateral side of the leg, is pathognomonic for KTS.

questions.question: What is the primary differentiator between Klippel-Trenaunay Syndrome and Parkes-Weber Syndrome?
choices: KTS is high flow; Parkes-Weber is low flow
KTS is low flow (venous); Parkes-Weber is high flow (arterial)
KTS is only seen in adults
questions.detail: KTS is a low-flow venous malformation, while Parkes-Weber is a true high-flow arterial malformation.

questions.question: An Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) of less than 0.3 is generally associated with which clinical symptom?
questions.detail: According to standard generalizations, an ABI < 0.3 correlates with rest pain.

questions.question: Why might a diabetic patient have a falsely elevated ABI (e.g., > 1.3)?
choices: They have high systemic blood pressure
Dense vascular calcifications prevent vessel compression
questions.detail: Arterial calcifications make compression difficult, leading to a false elevation of the ABI.

questions.question: If a patient with a threatened leg has an occluded ipsilateral common femoral artery (CFA), which access route is typically chosen?
questions.detail: Contralateral CFA is used when the ipsilateral CFA is occluded or if the patient is very obese.

questions.question: In the context of arterial access, what does "Antegrade access" mean?
choices: Access against the flow of blood
questions.detail: The nomenclature for downward access, towards the toes, is "antegrade".

questions.question: Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome is caused by blunt trauma to which vessel?
questions.detail: It is caused by blunt trauma to the ulnar artery and superficial palmar arch, often from using the hand as a hammer.

questions.question: What is a characteristic angiographic finding of Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome?
choices: String of beads in the forearm
Corkscrew configuration or pseudoaneurysm of the ulnar artery
Total occlusion of the radial artery
Dilation of the internal mammary artery
questions.detail: Hallmarks include corkscrew configuration of the superficial palmar arch, occlusion of the ulnar artery, or a pseudoaneurysm.

questions.question: Which landmark defines the transition where the Axillary Artery becomes the Brachial Artery?
choices: Lower border of the teres minor
Lower border of the teres major
questions.detail: The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major.

questions.question: Which leg artery is felt at the medial malleolus and is considered the most medial?
questions.detail: The posterior tibial artery is the most medial artery in the leg and is felt at the medial malleolus.

questions.question: Intimal Hyperplasia common causes re-stenosis during what timeframe after angioplasty?
choices: It is only seen in native vessels
questions.detail: Re-stenosis occurring 3-12 months after angioplasty is probably from intimal hyperplasia.

questions.question: Which syndrome is associated with a "Persistent Sciatic Artery"?
questions.detail: A persistent sciatic vein (and sometimes artery) is often associated with Klippel-Trenaunay Syndrome.

questions.question: What is the classic clinical history that suggests a persistent sciatic artery?
choices: External iliac is acutely occluded, but there is still a strong pulse in the foot
questions.detail: This occurs because the persistent sciatic artery is a continuation of the internal iliac that bypasses the external iliac system.

questions.question: In the "Acute Limb" triage, a category 3 "Irreversible" limb is characterized by:
choices: Complete muscle paralysis and complete sensory loss
Presence of an arterial Doppler signal
questions.detail: A category 3 limb is not salvageable and presents with complete paralysis and sensory loss.

questions.question: For an "Acute Limb" with combined inflow and outflow disease, which should be treated first?
questions.detail: If there is combined disease, you should treat the inflow first.

questions.question: If a limb has been occluded for less than 14 days, which treatment is generally considered superior?
questions.detail: If occluded for less than 14 days, thrombolysis is superior; if more than 14 days, surgery is superior.

questions.question: "Phlegmasia cerulea dolens" is a condition characterized by:
choices: Complete thrombosis of the deep venous system causing ischemia
questions.detail: This "painful blue leg" represents extreme venous obstruction that compromises arterial flow.

questions.question: What is the Wells Score primarily used to predict?
choices: Risk of stroke during carotid stenting
Clinical probability of DVT or PE
Success rate of a TIPS procedure
questions.detail: The Wells Score is used to stratify the risk of venous thromboembolism.

questions.question: Which ulcer location is classically associated with "Venous Stasis"?
questions.detail: Ulcers on the medial ankle are characteristic of venous stasis.

questions.question: An ulcer on the "Plantar (Sole) Surface" of the foot is classic for:
questions.detail: Neurotrophic ulcers typically involve the plantar surface.

questions.question: Post Thrombotic Syndrome (PTS) is scored using which system?
questions.detail: VEINES-QOL is the system used to classify the severity of PTS.

questions.question: What is the preferred vessel for a PICC line?
questions.detail: For PICC lines, the order of preference is basilic > brachial > cephalic.

questions.question: Why is the right internal jugular (RIJ) the preferred access site for a dialysis catheter?
choices: It is the largest vein in the body
It is the shortest, most direct route to the heart
It is easier to compress than the arm veins
questions.detail: The RIJ is preferred because it provides the shortest route and thromboses less than the subclavian.

questions.question: Which vasculitis is often referred to as "the pulseless disease"?
questions.detail: Takayasu Arteritis is the classic "pulseless disease".

questions.question: Takayasu Arteritis has a strong demographic preference for which group?
questions.detail: It characteristically affects young Asian girls.

questions.question: In the acute phase of Takayasu Arteritis, CT characteristically shows:
choices: Wall thickening and enhancement of the aorta
Multiple microaneurysms of the renal arteries
questions.detail: In the acute phase, wall thickening and enhancement are seen in the involved aorta.

questions.question: Which type of Takayasu Arteritis is the most common?
questions.detail: Type 3 is the most common and involves the arch and abdominal aorta.

questions.question: Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) is most common in which age group?
questions.detail: GCA is most common in elderly patients aged 70-80.

questions.question: GCA characteristically involves the temporal artery and which other region?
questions.detail: It involves the temporal artery and vessels in the "armpit" region (Subclavian, Axillary, Brachial).

questions.question: What is a classic clinical clue for GCA involvement of the axillary arteries?
choices: Part of the body compressed by crutches
questions.detail: GCA is linked to the "crutches" region (armpit vessels) in old men.

questions.question: What is the clinical relationship between GCA and "polymyalgia rheumatica"?
choices: Polymyalgia rheumatica is a side effect of treatment
They may be different phases of the same disease
They are only linked in Asian patients
questions.detail: They are clinically connected and may be different phases of the same disease.

questions.question: Which Location Strategy distinguishes central aortic involvement in vasculitis?
questions.detail: Central aortic involvement favors Takayasu.

questions.question: Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN) is more common in:
questions.detail: PAN is noted as being more common in men.

questions.question: What are the "Big 3" organs affected by Polyarteritis Nodosa?
questions.detail: PAN typically affects the kidneys (90%), heart (70%), and GI tract (50-70%).

questions.question: The hallmark imaging finding of PAN is the formation of:
questions.detail: Typically, PAN is characterized by microaneurysm formation, primarily at branch points.

questions.question: Which viral infection has a strong clinical association with PAN?
questions.detail: There is a known high-yield association between PAN and Hepatitis B.

questions.question: A "speed kidney" on a renal angiogram can be seen in PAN and also in users of:
questions.detail: Microaneurysm formation in the kidney is seen in patients who abuse crystal meth.

questions.question: What is the most common vasculitis in children?
questions.detail: Kawasaki is cited as one of the most common pediatric vasculitides.

questions.question: Coronary artery aneurysms in Kawasaki disease are considered "Giant" if they measure greater than:
questions.detail: Coronary aneurysms > 8 mm are considered "Giant" and high risk for MI.

questions.question: Wegener Granulomatosis primarily affects which areas?
questions.detail: Wegener's affects the upper/lower respiratory tract and the kidneys.

questions.question: Which finding in Wegener's is described as "like a cocaine addict"?
questions.detail: Nasal perforation is a classic way Wegener's is shown on exams.

questions.question: Churg-Strauss patients always have which two clinical features?
questions.detail: Churg-Strauss is always associated with asthma and eosinophilia.

questions.question: Cavitation of lung lesions is common in Wegener's but rare in:
questions.detail: Cavitation is rare in Churg-Strauss, distinguishing it from Wegener's.

questions.question: Microscopic Polyangiitis involves the kidneys and lungs, with 1/3 of cases demonstrating:
questions.detail: Diffuse pulmonary hemorrhage is the classic testable feature.

questions.question: In Henoch-Schonlein Purpura (HSP), which symptoms are most common?
questions.detail: GI symptoms and intussusception are common findings in kids with HSP.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of idiopathic scrotal edema in a child?
questions.detail: HSP is the most common cause of idiopathic scrotal edema.

questions.question: Behcet Disease is classically associated with which triad?
choices: Turkish descent, mouth ulcers, and genital ulcers
Asian descent, pulselessness, and aortic dilation
questions.detail: Mouth and genital ulcers in someone of Turkish descent are key buzzwords.

questions.question: What is the most likely question regarding vascular involvement in Behcet's?
questions.detail: Behcet's is a known high-yield cause of pulmonary artery aneurysms.

questions.question: Buerger Disease (Thromboangiitis Obliterans) is exclusively seen in:
questions.detail: Buerger disease is strongly and exclusively associated with smokers.

questions.question: What is the classic buzzword for the clinical outcome of Buerger Disease?
questions.detail: Buerger's leads to extensive occlusive disease and "auto-amputation".

questions.question: On a hand angiogram, what find identifies Buerger Disease versus Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome (HHS)?
choices: Buerger's involves the ulnar artery; HHS does not
Buerger's spares the ulnar artery but has corkscrew collaterals to fingers
There is no way to tell them apart
questions.detail: In Buerger's, the ulnar artery is generally spared while digits have occlusions and corkscrew collaterals.

questions.question: Cogan Syndrome presents with which unique combination?
questions.detail: Cogan syndrome presents with eye (optic neuritis/uveitis) and ear (audiovestibular) symptoms.

questions.question: Moyamoya Disease is characterized by progressive stenosis of which vessel?
questions.detail: Moyamoya involves progressive stenosis of the supraclinoid internal carotid artery.

questions.question: What is the angiographic buzzword for Moyamoya?
questions.detail: The "puff of smoke" appearance is due to the enlargement of basal perforating arteries.

questions.question: In a child with Moyamoya, what underlying condition should you suspect?
questions.detail: Moyamoya in a child is highly associated with sickle cell disease.

questions.question: How does the clinical presentation of Moyamoya differ between children and adults?
choices: Children have MI, adults have renal failure
questions.detail: Children typically present with stroke, whereas adults present with hemorrhage.

questions.question: Segmental Arterial Mediolysis (SAM) targets which vessels?
questions.detail: SAM targets the splanchnic arteries of the elderly and coronaries of young adults.

questions.question: What is the normal peak systolic velocity (PSV) for the internal carotid artery (ICA)?
questions.detail: A PSV < 125 cm/s is considered normal or indicative of no significant stenosis.

questions.question: According to NASCET criteria, what PSV defines "Moderate" (50-69%) ICA stenosis?
questions.detail: A PSV between 125 and 230 cm/s indicates 50-69% stenosis.

questions.question: What PSV defines "Severe" (>70%) ICA stenosis?
questions.detail: A PSV greater than 230 cm/s is the threshold for >70% stenosis.

questions.question: Besides PSV, which parameter indicates severe (>70%) carotid stenosis?
choices: ICA/CCA PSV ratio > 4.0 and ICA EDV > 100
Absence of a temporal tap reaction
questions.detail: Severe stenosis criteria include an ICA/CCA ratio > 4.0 and an EDV > 100.

questions.question: What type of waveform is found downstream from a significant proximal stenosis?
questions.detail: Tardus parvus is found downstream from a stenosis.

questions.question: If you see unilateral tardus parvus in the right carotid system, where is the stenosis?
questions.detail: Unilateral tardus parvus indicates a stenosis in the innominate (brachiocephalic) artery.

questions.question: If you see bilateral tardus parvus in the carotid arteries, what is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Bilateral tardus parvus points to a central problem, specifically the aortic valve.

questions.question: What is the hallmark finding of "Subclavian Steal" on Doppler?
choices: Retrograde flow in the ipsilateral vertebral artery
Reversal of flow in the CCA
"To and fro" flow in the aorta
questions.detail: Subclavian steal is defined by retrograde flow in the vertebral artery.

questions.question: What spectral waveform morphology is described as "mid-systolic deceleration" in early steal?
questions.detail: The "early steal" waveform looks like a rabbit.

questions.question: How can you distinguish the ICA from the ECA using vascular resistance?
choices: ICA is high resistance; ECA is low resistance
ICA is low resistance; ECA is high resistance
ECA has higher diastolic flow than the ICA
questions.detail: The brain (ICA) needs continuous diastolic flow, face muscles (ECA) do not.

questions.question: Which vessel of the carotid system characteristically has branches in the neck?
questions.detail: The external carotid has branches; the cervical ICA normally does not.

questions.question: The "Temporal Tap" technique identifies which vessel?
questions.detail: Tapping the temporal artery produces ripples in the ECA spectrum.

questions.question: What does a "loss of diastolic flow" suggest on a carotid Doppler study in an ICU patient?
questions.detail: A loss of diastolic flow suggests cerebral blood flow has ceased.

questions.question: "Aortic Regurgitation" characteristically shows:
questions.detail: Bilateral reversal of flow in the CCAs is the hallmark of aortic regurgitation.

questions.question: What is the "Pulsus Bisferiens" and when is it seen?
choices: Single large systolic peak in aortic stenosis
Double systolic peak seen in aortic regurgitation or HOCM
Loss of all flow in brain death
High resistance spike in the ICA
questions.detail: This is a double systolic peak seen in aortic regurgitation or hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy.

questions.question: Where is an "Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump" (IABP) correctly positioned?
choices: Proximal descending aorta, 2 cm distal to the left subclavian
questions.detail: The balloon should be in the proximal descending aorta, below the left subclavian.

questions.question: How does an IABP affect the ICA waveform?
choices: It creates a tardus parvus look
It creates an assisted peak in early diastole
questions.detail: The pump creates an "assisted peak" in early diastole.

questions.question: When measuring velocity in a patient with an IABP, which peak should be used?
questions.detail: You should measure the first peak, which is not assisted by the pump.

questions.question: What does the carotid Doppler look like with a "Left Ventricular Assist Device" (LVAD)?
questions.detail: LVAD waveforms become mostly flat because flow is continuous.

questions.question: In ICA Occlusion, what does the CCA waveform look like?
choices: It looks like a normal ICA
It shows massive "to and fro" flow
questions.detail: The CCA now only feeds the high-resistance ECA system.

questions.question: According to NASCET, how should the degree of carotid stenosis be measured?
choices: Comparing stenosis to the carotid bulb
Comparing narrowest part to a parallel segment of distal ICA
Measuring velocity at the bifurcation only
questions.detail: The rule is to use comparison to the normal distal vessel.

questions.question: At what level does the CCA bifurcation usually occur?
questions.detail: The bifurcation usually occurs at the C3-C4 level.

questions.question: A "Crescent Sign" of high T1 signal in the carotid wall is diagnostic for:
questions.detail: The T1 bright "crescent sign" represents intramural blood in a dissection.

questions.question: Blunt trauma in the neck tends to favor injury to which vessels?
questions.detail: Blunt trauma tends to favor the vertebral arteries.

questions.question: What is the ideal angle of insonation for Doppler?
questions.detail: The ideal angle is less than 60 degrees because strength follows the cosine of the angle.

questions.question: If a patient with a known LBBB needs a thoracic angiogram, what must be done first?
questions.detail: A catheter can cause a RBBB, and RBBB + LBBB = asystole.

questions.question: What is the most common vascular variant in posterior brain circulation?
questions.detail: Fetal PCA is seen in 30% of the population.

questions.question: A "Tau Sign" on sagittal MRI is characteristic of:
questions.detail: The tau sign represents a persistent fetal connection between the ICA and basilar artery.

questions.question: Which vascular pathology presents with acute CN3 palsy?
questions.detail: A PCOM aneurysm is the classic cause for CN3 palsy due to proximity.

questions.question: "Hoarseness" after a vascular procedure should make you worry about:
questions.detail: Hoarseness points to the recurrent laryngeal nerve being affected.

questions.question: What is the "Gold Standard" for diagnosing GCA?
questions.detail: Biopsy is the gold standard, though often negative.

questions.question: Which vasculitis is associated with cavitary lung lesions?
questions.detail: Wegener's is the classic cause of cavitary lung nodules among vasculitides.

questions.question: If an angiogram shows "bizarre vascularity" in a renal mass, what is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Bizarre vascularity is the classic description for RCC.
